rawFindings.description:
Sent 10 concurrent GET requests to /features/bookkeeping/.
- 10/10 requests succeeded (expected at most 1)

This may allow double-spend, coupon reuse, vote manipulation, or other TOCTOU attacks.
The endpoint should use database-level locks, idempotency keys, or optimistic concurrency control.

interpretedFindings.title: DOM-Based Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via URL Fragment
interpretedFindings.description: The homepage directly writes URL fragment content into the DOM via innerHTML without sanitization. An attacker can craft a malicious URL containing an HTML/JS payload in the fragment (e.g., #) and trick a user into clicking it, causing arbitrary JavaScript execution in the victim's browser.
interpretedFindings.impact: An attacker can steal session cookies, perform actions on behalf of the user, redirect to phishing pages, or exfiltrate sensitive financial data visible in the Moneybird UI. This is particularly severe for an accounting/finance application.
reproductionSteps: Observe the alert dialog firing, confirming JavaScript execution
The payload reaches at least two separate innerHTML sinks on the page
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Never assign unsanitized URL fragment data to innerHTML. Use textContent for plain text, or sanitize with DOMPurify before any HTML insertion. Audit all code paths that read from window.location.hash.

interpretedFindings.title: Missing Content-Security-Policy Header
interpretedFindings.description: The application does not set a Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header on any response. Without CSP, the browser has no instruction to block inline scripts, unauthorized external script sources, or data exfiltration — making the confirmed DOM XSS finding significantly more dangerous and easier to exploit.
interpretedFindings.impact: Absence of CSP removes a critical defense-in-depth layer. The existing DOM XSS vulnerability can be exploited without any browser-level mitigation. Attackers can load external scripts, exfiltrate data to arbitrary domains, and perform UI redressing.
reproductionSteps: Observe that no 'content-security-policy' header is present in the response
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Deploy a strict CSP header. Start with a report-only policy to identify violations before enforcing. Use nonces for inline scripts rather than 'unsafe-inline'. Set this header at the web server or CDN layer for all responses.

interpretedFindings.title: postMessage Handlers Missing Origin Validation
interpretedFindings.description: The homepage registers 3 postMessage event listeners that do not validate event.origin before processing the message. Any window with a reference to this page (e.g., a popup opener, iframe embedder, or cross-origin frame) can send arbitrary messages that will be processed without authentication of the sender.
interpretedFindings.impact: Depending on what the handlers do with received data, this could enable cross-origin state manipulation, token theft, triggering of privileged actions, or serving as an amplification vector for the existing XSS. Automated testing could not fully enumerate all handler logic, so manual review is warranted.
reproductionSteps: From a different origin (e.g., attacker.com), open a reference to the Moneybird window
Observe whether the handlers process the data and what side effects occur
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Always validate event.origin against an allowlist of trusted origins at the top of every postMessage handler. Reject messages from unexpected origins immediately.

interpretedFindings.title: Mixed Content: HTTP Resource Loaded on HTTPS Page
interpretedFindings.description: The HTTPS homepage references an HTTP URL (http://www.moneybird.com/artikelen/). This mixed content means that resource is transmitted over an unencrypted connection, undermining the TLS protection of the page. Modern browsers may block this, but it represents a misconfiguration that should be corrected.
interpretedFindings.impact: A network-level attacker (e.g., on the same Wi-Fi) could intercept or tamper with the HTTP resource via a man-in-the-middle attack. If the resource is a script, this could lead to full page compromise. Even for non-script resources, it degrades user trust and may trigger browser security warnings.
reproductionSteps: Visit https://www.moneybird.com/ and open browser DevTools → Console
Observe mixed content warnings referencing http://www.moneybird.com/artikelen/
Alternatively, inspect page source for http:// references
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Replace all hardcoded http:// internal URLs with https:// or protocol-relative URLs (//). Additionally, enable the 'upgrade-insecure-requests' CSP directive to automatically upgrade any remaining HTTP subresource requests to HTTPS.
interpretedFindings.codeExample: Articles Articles Articles Content-Security-Policy: upgrade-insecure-requests;

interpretedFindings.title: False Positive: Race Condition Finding on Static GET Endpoint
interpretedFindings.description: The scanner flagged /features/bookkeeping/ as vulnerable to race conditions because 10 concurrent GET requests all returned HTTP 200. However, this is a public, read-only marketing/feature page. GET requests to static or read-only content are inherently idempotent and returning 200 for all concurrent requests is correct, expected behavior — not a vulnerability.
interpretedFindings.impact: No real-world impact. This finding does not represent an exploitable race condition. True race condition risks (double-spend, coupon reuse, duplicate transactions) would only apply to authenticated state-mutating POST/PUT endpoints in the application backend.
reproductionSteps: This finding is assessed as a false positive and does not require reproduction
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: No fix required for this specific URL. Ensure that authenticated, state-mutating endpoints (e.g., payment processing, invoice creation, subscription changes) use database-level transactions, idempotency keys, or optimistic locking to prevent genuine race conditions in the application core.

topIssues: Fix DOM XSS: Sanitize URL fragment data before writing to innerHTML — this is a confirmed, directly exploitable vulnerability on the homepage of a financial application
Deploy Content-Security-Policy header: A strict CSP would have blocked the DOM XSS exploit and provides defense-in-depth against future injection issues
Add origin validation to all postMessage handlers: Prevents cross-origin message injection which could compound the XSS risk or enable independent state manipulation attacks
